---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size60[.center[

Paper 1: Writing Workshop

]]

<br>

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. ...



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-green_blue_swirl_side.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# Paper 1

.size40[
**Make an argument that a specific, local environmental issue is a collective choice problem** (e.g. it is a problem for our community and it should be addressed collectively, not privately). 

Be sure to specify the data that supports your argument (don't ask anyone to take your word for it) and the stakeholders in our community most relevant to the problem.
]

???

Always good to start with the prompt for the assignment.

As you write and revise, remember that your paper should represent a logical and coherent answer to this question.

### Questions on the assignment prompt?

<br>

Working together we devised an outline that I think sets up your projects in a really compelling way.

SLIDE




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**Paper 1 Outline**]]

.size30[
Introduction

Why is this a "problem"?

Why should this be addressed "collectively" and not "privately"?

How can we measure the problem?

What is "good" progress on this problem? Can it be "fixed"?

Who are the relevant stakeholders?

Conclusion
]

???

A first paper that covers these elements in a convincing way, is a paper that sets you up really well to address a specific environmental problem. 





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**Paper 1 Outline**]]

.size30[
.textblue[**Introduction**]

Why is this a "problem"?

Why should this be addressed "collectively" and not "privately"?

How can we measure the problem?

What is "good" progress on this problem? Can it be "fixed"?

Who are the relevant stakeholders?

.textblue[**Conclusion**]
]

???

Your introduction and conclusion will book-end the paper, tying all of the premises into a single, coherent argument.

- Always keep one eye on the over-arching prompt: Make an argument that a specific, local environmental issue is a collective choice problem

- The intro and the conclusion help the reader connect all the work you do in the report into an answer to this question.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**Paper 1 Outline**]]

.size30[
Introduction

.textblue[
**Why is this a "problem"?**

**Why should this be addressed "collectively" and not "privately"?**

**How can we measure the problem?**

**What is "good" progress on this problem? Can it be "fixed"?**

**Who are the relevant stakeholders?**
]

Conclusion
]

???

Each of these items represent premises in your over-arching argument.

- Each requires you to make and support a sub-argument with evidence.

The sum of all these components should represent a thorough answer to the question: Is this environmental issue a collective choice problem?

### Questions on any of these? Clarifications needed?

<br>

#### Elements we discussed previously
- A good introduction: What is the question? Why should we care? What is your argument? (Thesis Statement)

- Stakeholder examples
    - Who, or what kinds of groups, "caused" the problem?
    - Who, or what kinds of groups, are "impacted" by the problem?
    - Who, or what kinds of groups, would make good allies in your work on the problem? (All the better if they are already working on this problem)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**For Today: Feedback on Outlines**]]

.size30[
Introduction

Why is this a "problem"?

Why should this be addressed "collectively" and not "privately"?

How can we measure the problem?

What is "good" progress on this problem? Can it be "fixed"?

Who are the relevant stakeholders?

Conclusion
]

???

### Everybody ready to go with at least some notes under each of these premises?

<br>

Ok, let's split off into pairs and take some time to dive into the outlines.

To start, 10 minutes, person 1 walk the other person through your outline and get feedback.

<br>

Swap, 10 minutes for the other person to take the lead and present their outline to get feedback.

<br>

Ok, new pairs and let's do this again and then we'll all talk as a group
- 10 mins, person 1
- 10 mins, person 2



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size50[**For Today: Feedback on Outlines**]]

.size30[
Introduction

Why is this a "problem"?

Why should this be addressed "collectively" and not "privately"?

How can we measure the problem?

What is "good" progress on this problem? Can it be "fixed"?

Who are the relevant stakeholders?

Conclusion
]

???

### Ok, how are we doing?

#### Things to clarify?

#### Elements you can steal from other peoples' best ideas?

#### What do you still need to work on most?

<br>

Before we adjourn, preview of next week!



---

class: middle, slideblue

# Semester in Three Sections 

.size45[
1. How do we approach environmental politics? (4 wks)

2. **What are the complicating factors in environmental policymaking?** (5 wks)

3. Designing an environmental policy (6 wks)
]

???

Next week we dive into section 2 of the semester.

We'll be deepening your analysis of your problem in terms of:
- Risk aversion
- Uncertainty
- Collective action problems, and
- Inequality

<br>

Make sure to finish up those papers and get them submitted on Moodle by the deadline next week.
